metadata.description: Solibri-based rule library for VLM visual compliance checking. Focused on rules that can be evaluated through visual inspection of IFC/BIM model snapshots.

rules.title: 207 Accessible Ramp Rule
rules.description: Verifies ramp accessibility by checking slope/gradient, width, handrail presence and configuration, landing dimensions, and clear floor space. Ensures ramps comply with accessibility standards.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Ramp slope is visually assessable, handrails are clearly visible, and landing presence can be confirmed. VLM can evaluate relative steepness and identify safety features.
visualAspects: Ramp slope visually apparent
Handrail presence observable
Landing size and position visible
Ramp width measurable
Edge protection (curbs) visible
limitations: Exact slope percentage difficult without measurements
Handrail height precise measurement challenging
Very gentle slopes (<5%) hard to distinguish from level surfaces

lookFor: Ramp incline and slope
Ramp width
Handrails on one or both sides
Landing at top and bottom
Intermediate landings for long ramps
Edge protection/curbs
Clear floor space at ramp ends
passIndicators: Gentle visible slope (appears <8%)
Handrails present on both sides
Level landings at transitions
Adequate ramp width
Edge protection visible
failIndicators: Steep slope (visibly >8%)
Missing handrails
No landing at top or bottom
Ramp too narrow
Missing edge protection on drop-offs
uncertainIndicators: Slope appears borderline, cannot determine exact percentage
Unclear if handrails meet height requirements
Landing size unclear from current view
Cannot see full ramp length

pass: Ramp slope appears gentle (consistent with 1:12 or less)
Handrails present and appear continuous
Level landings visible at top, bottom, and intermediate points
Adequate width for wheelchair passage
fail: Ramp slope appears steep (inconsistent with 1:12 max)
Handrails absent or discontinuous
Missing required landings
Ramp width appears insufficient
uncertain: Slope percentage not determinable from current view
Need side elevation to assess slope properly
Handrail configuration unclear
Landing dimensions not fully visible

tips: Use side elevation to assess slope angle
Front view shows handrail presence
Zoom to check landing levelness
Orbit to verify handrails on both sides

findingTemplates.pass: Ramp meets accessibility standards. Slope appears compliant, handrails present, landings adequate.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully assess ramp compliance. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

rules.notes: Maximum slope 1:12 for new construction. Steeper slopes (up to 1:8) may be allowed for existing facilities with space constraints. Handrail extensions required at top and bottom.

rules.title: 208 Accessible Door Rule
rules.description: Verifies door accessibility from different perspectives, including dimensions, glazing ratios, opening directions, and free spaces around the door.

source.version: not stated in dedicated help article

visualSuitability.reasoning: Door clearances are highly visible in plan and 3D views. VLM can assess free space around doors, identify obstructing elements (walls, furniture), measure relative distances, and evaluate door swing geometry.
visualAspects: Door opening geometry is visible
Surrounding free space can be assessed
Obstructing elements (walls, furniture, equipment) are visible
Door width relative to opening is measurable
Approach angles and maneuvering space are observable
limitations: Exact measurements may require reference dimensions
Door operation type (push/pull/sliding) may need metadata
Movable furniture vs fixed obstacles may be ambiguous

lookFor: Door opening and its surrounding floor area
Clear floor space on pull side (typically 60" x door width + 18")
Clear floor space on push side (typically 48" x door width)
Objects or walls that may obstruct maneuvering
Door swing arc and potential conflicts
Latch side clearance (typically 18" minimum)
passIndicators: Ample clear floor space visible on both sides of door
No obstructions within required clearance zones
Door can swing fully open without collision
Adequate space for wheelchair maneuvering visible
failIndicators: Walls, columns, or furniture blocking door clearance
Insufficient space for door to swing open
Corridor or room too narrow at door location
Objects placed immediately in front of door
uncertainIndicators: Unclear whether objects are movable or fixed
Door opening direction not evident from view
Cannot determine exact clearance dimensions from current view
Partial occlusion of door area

pass: Visible clear floor space on both sides meets minimum dimensions
No fixed obstructions within required clearance zones
Door swing path is clear of conflicts
fail: Fixed obstructions (walls, columns) within required clearance
Insufficient floor area for wheelchair maneuvering
Door swing blocked by walls or fixed elements
uncertain: Door operation type unclear
Ambiguous whether objects are movable
Current view does not show full clearance area
Need different angle or plan view to assess properly

tips: Use plan view to assess clearance dimensions
Isolate doors and surrounding elements
Check both sides of door with camera rotation
Use plan cut at wheelchair seat height for better context

findingTemplates.pass: Door has adequate maneuvering clearance. Clear floor space visible on both pull and push sides with no obstructions.
findingTemplates.fail: Door clearance insufficient. {obstruction_type} obstructs the required {clearance_side} clearance zone, preventing wheelchair maneuvering.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully assess door clearance from current view. {reason}. Recommend {suggested_action}.

rules.notes: This rule is based on ADA and ICC A117.1 standards. Clearance requirements vary based on approach direction (front, latch, hinge) and whether door has a closer and latch.

rules.title: 209 Free Floor Space
rules.description: Checks accessibility-related free floor space in selected spaces, such as unobstructed corridor width, wheelchair turning space, and clearance zones needed for access and maneuvering.

source.version: not stated in dedicated help article

visualSuitability.reasoning: Free floor space, turning areas, and corridor clearances are strongly visible in plan and plan-cut views. A VLM can assess whether open floor zones are occupied by walls, fixtures, furniture, or other modeled elements.
visualAspects: Open floor area is visible in plan view
Turning-circle zones can be approximated visually
Corridor pinch points are visible
Furniture or fixtures intruding into clear space are visible
Access space next to beds, sanitary fixtures, or doors can be inspected
limitations: Exact compliance depends on rule parameters and reference dimensions
Movable versus fixed objects may be ambiguous
Overlapping clearances may require metadata or measured geometry

lookFor: Unobstructed corridor width
Wheelchair turning space inside rooms
Open floor space next to fixtures, beds, or equipment
Walls, furniture, or components intruding into required clear areas
Pinch points or dead-end maneuvering zones
passIndicators: Continuous open floor space is visible
Turning area appears unobstructed
Corridor width appears consistently clear
Approach space to the relevant object or area appears usable
failIndicators: Furniture or fixtures block turning or approach space
Corridor width is visibly narrowed by obstructions
Required free floor zone is occupied by fixed elements
Access beside the target object appears pinched or blocked
uncertainIndicators: Exact free-space dimensions cannot be confirmed from the current view
Partial occlusion hides room corners or clearance edges
It is unclear whether the object causing the obstruction is movable
The intended checked use case is unclear without rule parameters

pass: Required free floor space appears unobstructed
Turning or approach space appears continuous and usable
No obvious fixed obstruction intrudes into the required clear area
fail: A fixed object clearly intrudes into the required free floor area
Turning or maneuvering space is visibly insufficient
The circulation zone is clearly obstructed or pinched
uncertain: The relevant free-space requirement depends on project-specific parameters
A dimensioned or top view is needed to verify compliance
The current evidence does not show the full required clearance area

tips: Use plan view first to inspect clear floor areas
Check the narrowest points of the space
Inspect turning areas around fixtures and inside bathrooms
Hide upper elements if they visually clutter floor-space assessment

findingTemplates.pass: Required free floor space appears adequate and unobstructed for the checked accessibility condition.
findingTemplates.fail: Free floor space is insufficient. {obstruction_type} intrudes into the required {zone_type}.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify free floor space. {reason}. Recommend {suggested_action}.

dimensionalRequirements.referenceStandard: Explicit example on Solibri Rule 209 page
Explicit example on Solibri Rule 209 page

dimensionalRequirements.typicalValue: 60 inches x 60 inches overall minimum, arms/base 36 inches minimum

dimensionalRequirements.parameter: Clear Floor or Ground Space (ADA reference)

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri Rule 209 documentation describes this as a configurable rule for different accessibility-related free-space checks and gives examples of 1.2 m unobstructed corridor width and a 1.5 m wheelchair turning circle. The ADA values listed here are external reference values for visual interpretation and should be treated as code references, not as fixed Solibri defaults. Spaces must be classified by usage to use the Solibri rule.

rules.title: 210 Accessible Stair Rule
rules.description: Checks staircase accessibility including riser height consistency, tread depth uniformity, stair width, handrail presence and height, landing dimensions, and headroom clearance. Ensures stairs are safe and accessible.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Stair geometry is highly visual - riser heights, tread depths, handrails, and landings are all observable. VLM can assess consistency, count steps, verify handrail presence, and check relative proportions.
visualAspects: Riser heights are visually comparable
Tread depths can be assessed
Handrail presence and continuity observable
Landing size and position visible
Stair width measurable relative to reference
Headroom clearance visible in side views
limitations: Precise measurements (e.g., 7.5" max riser) difficult without scale
Handrail height exact measurement challenging
Tactile indicators may be too small to see clearly

lookFor: Individual stair risers and treads
Consistency of riser heights across flight
Uniformity of tread depths
Handrails on one or both sides
Handrail height above nosing
Handrail extensions beyond top and bottom
Landing presence and size
Clear headroom above stair
Nosing profile and projection
passIndicators: Risers appear uniform in height
Treads appear consistent in depth
Handrails present on both sides
Handrails appear at appropriate height
Handrails extend beyond stairs
Landings present between flights
No visible obstructions in headroom
failIndicators: Visible variation in riser heights
Irregular tread depths
Missing handrails
Handrails too high or too low
Handrail discontinuity or gaps
Missing landings between flights
Beams or soffits intruding into headroom
uncertainIndicators: Cannot see all steps in current view
Handrail height unclear from angle
Headroom obstruction not visible from this view
Unclear if landings meet size requirements

pass: All visible risers appear uniform (within typical 3/8" tolerance)
Treads appear consistent throughout flight
Handrails present and continuous
Landings present where required
Clear headroom visible above stair path
fail: Obvious variation in riser heights
Missing or discontinuous handrails
No landing between long flights
Visible headroom obstruction (beams, ductwork, etc.)
uncertain: Cannot see entire stair flight in current view
Viewing angle prevents assessment of handrail height
Need plan cut or different view to verify headroom
Tread/riser dimensions unclear without reference scale

tips: Use side view to assess riser/tread consistency
Front view helps verify handrail presence
Orbit camera to check handrails on both sides
Zoom to individual steps to check geometry
Check headroom with camera positioned below stairs looking up

findingTemplates.pass: Staircase meets accessibility requirements. Risers uniform, treads consistent, handrails present and continuous, adequate headroom observed.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify stair compliance from current view. {limitation}. Suggest {action}.

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri rule checks stairs, landings, handrails, and tactile ground surface indicators (TGSI). Stairs must be classified in the Vertical Access classification. Dimensional thresholds are defined through rule parameters and can follow local standards such as IBC.

rules.title: 226 Free Area in Front of Components
rules.description: Checks that required free area exists in front of selected components, ensuring sufficient space for access, operation, and maintenance.

source.solibriTitle: 226 Free Area in Front of Components
source.version: not stated in dedicated help article

visualSuitability.reasoning: Front clearance zones are strongly visible in plan and 3D views. A VLM can determine whether required access space in front of components is obstructed by walls, equipment, or furniture.
visualAspects: Component front face is visible
Clear space in front of component can be visually assessed
Obstructions such as walls, furniture, or equipment are visible
Relative depth of clearance area can be approximated
Multiple components and their interactions can be observed
limitations: Exact depth and width of free area depend on rule parameters
Component orientation may require IFC metadata
Front direction may not always be visually explicit
Movable vs fixed obstructions may be ambiguous

lookFor: Component front face (doors, panels, equipment, windows, cabinets)
Clear space directly in front of the component
Walls, furniture, or other components blocking access
Depth of usable space in front of the component
Overlap of clearance zones between adjacent components
passIndicators: Front area appears open and unobstructed
Sufficient space exists for approach and operation
No visible obstruction blocks access to the component
Clearance zone appears continuous
failIndicators: Object or wall directly blocks front access
Clearance area is visibly too shallow
Component is placed too close to another element
Overlapping elements reduce usable front space
uncertainIndicators: Front orientation of component unclear
Exact required clearance depth cannot be determined
Partial occlusion of clearance area
Unclear whether obstruction is permanent

pass: Required free area in front of the component appears unobstructed
Access to the component appears possible
Clearance zone appears continuous and usable
fail: Front clearance is visibly obstructed
Insufficient space for access or operation
Component cannot be approached due to obstruction
uncertain: Required clearance depends on rule parameters
Component orientation unclear
Need measurements or different view to confirm

tips: View the component orthogonally to understand front direction
Use plan view to assess depth of clearance
Inspect equipment rooms and service areas
Check for overlapping clearance zones

findingTemplates.pass: Required free area in front of the component appears adequate and unobstructed.
findingTemplates.fail: Front clearance is insufficient. {obstruction} blocks required access in front of the component.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify front clearance. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

dimensionalRequirements.parameter: Free Area in Front of Component
dimensionalRequirements.typicalValue: Configurable (depends on component type and rule settings)
Varies by system (e.g., electrical panels often ~36 inches / 915mm)
dimensionalRequirements.referenceStandard: Typical code practice (e.g., NEC / local regulations)

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri Rule 226 checks that sufficient free area exists in front of selected components. The size and orientation of the clearance area are defined through rule parameters and depend on component type. External reference values (e.g., ADA or electrical codes) are included for interpretation and are not fixed Solibri defaults.

rules.title: 236 Guarding Against Falling
rules.description: Checks that horizontal structures such as slabs, balconies, and platforms are protected against falling by walls, railings, or adjacent structures when a height difference creates a fall hazard.

source.solibriTitle: 236 Horizontal Structures Must Be Guarded Against Falling
source.version: not stated in dedicated help article

visualSuitability.reasoning: Edges of horizontal structures and presence or absence of guarding elements are highly visible in 3D and elevation views. A VLM can detect open edges, drops, and missing or incomplete barriers.
visualAspects: Horizontal slab edges are clearly visible
Height differences between adjacent levels are observable
Railings, walls, or parapets are visible
Open edges or voids can be identified
Continuity of guarding elements can be assessed
limitations: Exact fall height threshold depends on rule parameters
Guardrail height cannot be precisely measured without scale
Small gaps or openings in railings may be hard to detect
Requires 3D or elevation view for accurate assessment

lookFor: Edges of slabs, balconies, mezzanines, or platforms
Height differences between walking surface and adjacent lower level
Presence of railings, parapets, or walls at edges
Open sides without protection
Continuity of guarding elements along edges
passIndicators: All exposed edges appear protected by guardrails or walls
No visible open edge with significant drop
Guarding elements appear continuous along edge
Adjacent structures provide equivalent protection
failIndicators: Open edge with visible drop and no guarding
Missing railing or parapet at exposed edge
Discontinuity or gap in guarding element
Platform or balcony edge left unprotected
uncertainIndicators: Height difference unclear from current view
Guarding element partially occluded
Need different angle to confirm edge protection
Unclear if adjacent surface provides sufficient protection

pass: All exposed edges appear protected against falling
Guarding elements appear continuous and effective
No visible fall hazard along horizontal surfaces
fail: Exposed edge lacks visible guarding
Guarding is clearly incomplete or missing
Obvious fall hazard present at horizontal edge
uncertain: Fall height threshold cannot be determined visually
Need elevation or section view to confirm
Guarding adequacy unclear without measurements

tips: Orbit around slab and balcony edges
Inspect mezzanines and voids carefully
Check continuity of railings along edges
Use side view to understand height differences

findingTemplates.pass: Horizontal structure appears adequately protected against falling.
findingTemplates.fail: Fall protection issue detected. {location} has an exposed edge without sufficient guarding.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify fall protection. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri Rule 236 checks whether horizontal structures with a height difference are properly protected against falling by guardrails, walls, or adjacent structures. The minimum fall height and required guarding are defined through rule parameters. External reference values such as guardrail heights are based on building codes and are not fixed Solibri defaults.

rules.title: 246 Accessible Space
rules.description: Checks that required accessible spaces exist within selected spaces and that these spaces are reachable from the passageway, ensuring sufficient maneuvering and usability.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Accessible spaces and their connectivity to entrances are visually assessable in plan and 3D views. A VLM can determine whether required maneuvering zones exist and whether they are reachable without obstruction.
visualAspects: Clear maneuvering space inside rooms is visible
Access path from door to target space is visible
Obstructions within accessible zones are visible
Spatial relationships between entrance and target area are observable
Continuity of free space can be visually traced
limitations: Exact geometry of required space (cylinder/cuboid) depends on parameters
Accessibility requirements vary by use case
Connectivity may depend on IFC classification or semantics
Precise dimensions require scale reference

lookFor: Accessible space inside rooms (e.g., bathrooms, circulation zones)
Clear path from entrance or door to accessible space
Furniture or fixtures obstructing maneuvering areas
Continuity of free floor space
Entrances and their relation to accessible zones
passIndicators: Accessible space appears present and unobstructed
Continuous clear path exists from entrance to accessible area
No visible obstruction blocks maneuvering or access
Space appears usable for intended accessibility purpose
failIndicators: Required accessible space is missing or occupied
Path from entrance to accessible space is blocked
Furniture or fixtures obstruct required maneuvering area
Accessible space is isolated and not reachable
uncertainIndicators: Exact size or shape of accessible space unclear
Connectivity cannot be verified from current view
Partial occlusion of relevant areas
Need plan view or measurements to confirm

pass: Accessible space appears present and reachable from entrance
Required maneuvering area appears unobstructed
Continuous access path is visible
fail: Accessible space is missing, obstructed, or not reachable
No clear path connects entrance to accessible space
Required maneuvering area is clearly insufficient
uncertain: Accessibility depends on configured rule parameters
Exact dimensions or connectivity cannot be verified visually
Need plan view or measurements for confirmation

tips: Use plan view to verify accessible space and paths
Inspect entrance-to-space connectivity
Check maneuvering zones around fixtures
Ensure full visibility of the room footprint

findingTemplates.pass: Accessible space appears adequate and reachable from the entrance.
findingTemplates.fail: Accessible space requirement not met. {issue} prevents proper maneuvering or access.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify accessible space. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

dimensionalRequirements.typicalValue: Configurable (cylinder or cuboid depending on rule settings)
30 inches x 48 inches (760 mm x 1220 mm)

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri Rule 246 checks that required accessible space exists within a space and that it is reachable from the passageway. The space can be defined as a cylinder or cuboid depending on rule parameters. ADA values are included as external references and are not fixed Solibri defaults.

rules.title: 247 Local Accessible Circulation Rule
rules.description: Checks that corridors and circulation spaces have adequate width for accessible passage, identifies obstructions that narrow the path, and verifies clear floor space. Ensures wheelchair-accessible circulation.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Corridor width is highly visual and easily assessed from plan views. VLM can identify narrow points, measure relative widths, and detect obstructions like columns, furniture, or protruding elements.
visualAspects: Corridor width clearly visible in plan view
Obstructions (columns, furniture, equipment) easily identified
Narrow points visually obvious
Wall-to-wall or wall-to-obstruction distances measurable
Passing spaces observable
limitations: Exact measurements require reference dimensions
Overhanging elements may not be visible in plan view

lookFor: Corridor width between walls
Obstructions narrowing the path (columns, furniture, equipment)
Clear width after accounting for obstacles
Protruding elements (wall-mounted items, handrails)
Pinch points or bottlenecks
Door swings reducing corridor width
passIndicators: Corridor appears wide (visually >36 inches clear)
No obstructions in circulation path
Uniform width throughout length
Adequate passing spaces visible
failIndicators: Narrow corridor (visually <36 inches)
Columns or walls creating pinch points
Furniture blocking passage
Door swings reducing clear width below minimum
uncertainIndicators: Cannot determine exact width from current view
Unclear if objects are movable or fixed
Need plan view to assess properly
Protrusions not fully visible

pass: Clear width appears adequate (>36 inches) throughout corridor
No fixed obstructions narrowing the path
Pinch points absent or meet minimum width
fail: Corridor width clearly insufficient (<36 inches)
Fixed obstructions (columns, walls) creating narrow passage
Pinch point below accessible minimum
uncertain: Width borderline, need measurements
Unclear if obstructions are fixed or movable
Need plan view or different angle to assess

tips: Use plan view for best width assessment
Plan cut at waist height to see protruding elements
Isolate walls and obstructions
Measure at narrowest points
Check entire corridor length for pinch points

findingTemplates.pass: Corridor width is adequate. Clear width exceeds minimum requirements with no significant obstructions.
findingTemplates.fail: Corridor is too narrow. {location} has clear width of approximately {width}, below the {standard} minimum of {required_width}. Obstruction: {obstruction}.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot determine exact corridor width from current view. {issue}. Recommend {action}.

rules.notes: Mapped to SOL-247 Local Accessible Circulation, not the Solibri 209 Free Floor Space article. 36-inch minimum for accessible routes. 60-inch width required for two wheelchairs to pass. Protruding objects limited to 4 inches between 27-80 inches above floor.

rules.title: 248 Accessible Area around Objects
rules.description: Checks that required accessible areas exist around selected objects, such as in front of, behind, or beside components, ensuring sufficient clearance for approach and use.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Clearance zones around objects are highly visible in plan and 3D views. A VLM can detect whether required approach spaces are obstructed by walls, fixtures, or furniture.
visualAspects: Target object location is clearly visible
Front, side, and rear clearance zones can be visually approximated
Obstructions within clearance zones are visible
Relative spacing between objects and walls is observable
Accessible approach directions can be inferred
limitations: Exact clearance dimensions depend on rule parameters
Orientation of clearance zones may require metadata
Movable vs fixed objects may be ambiguous
Overlapping clearance zones may be difficult to interpret visually

lookFor: Target objects such as toilets, sinks, equipment, or doors
Clear floor space in front of, beside, or behind the object
Walls, fixtures, or furniture intruding into clearance zones
Available approach directions to the object
Spacing between adjacent objects
passIndicators: Clear space exists around the object in required directions
No visible obstruction within the accessible area
Object appears reachable and usable from at least one side
Clearance zone appears continuous and unobstructed
failIndicators: Wall or fixture blocks required side or front clearance
Object is tightly enclosed with no usable approach space
Clearance area is visibly occupied by another element
Access path to the object is obstructed
uncertainIndicators: Exact clearance dimensions cannot be verified visually
Orientation of required clearance is unclear
Partial occlusion of object or surrounding space
Unclear whether obstruction is permanent or movable

pass: Required accessible area around the object appears unobstructed
At least one valid approach direction appears usable
No visible fixed obstruction intrudes into the required clearance zone
fail: Required accessible area is visibly obstructed
Object cannot be approached or used due to surrounding elements
Clearance zone is clearly insufficient for accessibility
uncertain: Clearance requirements depend on object type and parameters
Need plan view or dimensions to verify exact compliance
Object orientation or clearance direction is unclear

tips: Use plan view to inspect clearance zones around objects
Check front and side access to fixtures
Zoom into bathrooms and equipment areas
Isolate relevant object categories for clarity

findingTemplates.pass: Accessible area around the object appears adequate and unobstructed.
findingTemplates.fail: Accessible area is insufficient. {obstruction} intrudes into the required {clearance_direction} clearance zone.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify accessible area. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

dimensionalRequirements.typicalValue: Configurable (depends on object type and rule settings)

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri Rule 248 checks accessible areas around objects in specified directions (front, side, back) based on configurable parameters. Clearance requirements depend on object type and accessibility standards. ADA values are included as external references for interpretation and are not fixed Solibri defaults.

rules.title: 250 Component Visibility
rules.description: Checks whether selected components are visible from defined viewpoints, ensuring that required elements can be seen for inspection, coordination, or design validation purposes.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Visibility is inherently a visual problem. A VLM can directly assess whether components are occluded, obstructed, or clearly visible from a given viewpoint or camera position.
visualAspects: Component presence in view is directly observable
Occlusion by walls, slabs, or other components is visible
Line-of-sight relationships can be inferred
Foreground/background relationships are clear
Partial vs full visibility can be distinguished
limitations: Exact viewpoint definition may depend on rule parameters
Hidden components behind transparent elements may be ambiguous
Occlusion may depend on camera position not represented in snapshot
Semantic importance of visibility may require metadata

lookFor: Target components in the scene
Obstructions between camera/viewpoint and component
Walls, slabs, or elements blocking line of sight
Partial vs full visibility of component geometry
Distance and angle affecting visibility
passIndicators: Component is clearly visible in the scene
No obstruction blocks line of sight
Component geometry is fully or sufficiently visible
Component can be visually inspected
failIndicators: Component is completely occluded
Line of sight is blocked by walls or other elements
Component is hidden behind other geometry
Only insignificant or no visible portion of component
uncertainIndicators: Component partially visible but insufficient for evaluation
Occlusion unclear due to camera angle
Need alternative viewpoint to confirm visibility
Transparency or rendering ambiguity

pass: Component is visible from the given viewpoint
No major obstruction blocks line of sight
Visibility is sufficient for inspection or validation
fail: Component is not visible due to obstruction
Line of sight is fully blocked
Component cannot be inspected visually
uncertain: Visibility depends on viewpoint configuration
Partial visibility insufficient for decision
Need alternative camera position or additional views

tips: Move camera to target component viewpoint
Check for occluding geometry between camera and object
Use multiple angles to confirm visibility
Hide interfering elements if necessary

findingTemplates.pass: Component is visible and can be inspected from the defined viewpoint.
findingTemplates.fail: Component is not visible. {obstruction} blocks the line of sight.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot fully verify component visibility. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

rules.notes: The dedicated Solibri Rule 250 evaluates whether components are visible from specified viewpoints based on rule configuration. It is not based on dimensional thresholds but on line-of-sight and occlusion logic. This rule is primarily used for quality assurance and inspection workflows.

rules.title: 252 Headroom Clearance
rules.description: Evaluates headroom clearance along accessible routes as part of accessible route compliance, focusing on vertical obstructions affecting circulation paths, stairs, ramps, and doorways.

visualSuitability.reasoning: Headroom issues are visually identifiable when beams, ductwork, or sloped ceilings intrude into circulation space. VLM can spot low-hanging elements and assess relative heights.
visualAspects: Overhead obstructions visible in elevation/3D views
Low beams or soffits apparent
Ductwork or mechanical elements observable
Sloped ceilings visible
Relative height assessment possible
limitations: Exact clearance height difficult to measure without reference
Small protrusions may not be visible
Requires elevation or 3D view, not visible in plan

lookFor: Overhead beams, joists, or structural elements
Ductwork, pipes, or mechanical systems
Sloped ceilings or soffits
Low-hanging light fixtures
Clearance above stairs and ramps
Clearance at doorways
passIndicators: Clear vertical space with no obstructions
Ceiling height appears adequate (>7 feet)
No low-hanging elements visible
Uniform clearance throughout path
failIndicators: Beams or ductwork protruding low into space
Sloped ceiling creating head strike hazard
Light fixtures or signage hanging too low
Reduced clearance under stairs
uncertainIndicators: Cannot assess clearance from current view angle
Need elevation view to verify
Unclear if element creates hazard without measurements
Partial occlusion prevents assessment

pass: No visible overhead obstructions
Clearance appears adequate (visually >80 inches)
Ceiling height uniform and sufficient
fail: Beam or ductwork clearly intrudes into required clearance
Sloped ceiling creates obvious head strike hazard
Clearance visibly insufficient (<80 inches)
uncertain: Cannot determine clearance height from plan view
Need elevation or section view
Element may or may not intrude, measurements needed

tips: Use elevation views to assess vertical clearance
Position camera at pedestrian eye height
Check under stairs and beams specifically
Rotate camera to look up at ceiling
Isolate structural and MEP elements

findingTemplates.pass: Headroom clearance is adequate. No overhead obstructions observed along circulation path.
findingTemplates.fail: Insufficient headroom clearance. {obstruction_type} at {location} reduces clearance to approximately {height}, below the minimum {required_height}.
findingTemplates.uncertain: Cannot verify headroom clearance from current view. {reason}. Recommend {action}.

rules.notes: 80-inch minimum clearance required for accessible routes. Lower clearance allowed for specific conditions (e.g., door frames). Protruding objects above 80 inches not restricted.
